title: podman machine init Support emulating different architectures
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->
It would be nice to be able to init a machine with different architecture type than the host machine. Should be supported through qemu. For example run x86 on arm or arm on x86.

Something like:
```
podman machine init --arch=x86_64 x86_machine
```

@rhatdan - see request and discussion at https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1088
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since this can be supported via installing an privileged image with qemu-user-static built in, shouldn't we just document how to do this and then we don't need fedora coreos to change.
Would be nice to have it also for macOS. @rhatdan

Yes we are working on this upstream right now,  basically trying to get qemu-user-static split into separate packages so we could install fewer of them on Fedora CoreOS.

https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2061584

Thank you for working on having the podman managed machine incorporate the QEMU emulation packages. Currently I have had to handle this in a restrictive corporate environment and specifically for an M1 Mac. By restrictive I mean:
1. End computing assets must be routed through a proxy server to access public / external / Internet resources.
2. Proxy infrastructure are SSL inspecting.

Combined this needs to be a consideration, unless, the multi-architecture support emulation is going to be packaged with the podman managed machine. The remainder of this assumes that the emulation support is not packaged with the podman managed machine. To that end, this is how one can get the necessary dependencies installed in the podman managed machine, in a restrictive corporate environment. Please note that none of this applies to a true disconnected environment.

The second part is trivial but due to an inspecting SSL proxy one must be able to provision those specific certificates for the inspection solution (e.g., Menlo Security as an example but has a ton of competitors with varying degrees of solution approaches).

For the first part, I had to configure the podman managed machine to use a proxy in order to install the **qemu-user-static** package (and yes, all of its dependencies) at this point in time.

Assuming one creates a simple configuration file defining the desired proxy configuration, entitled simply as **proxy.env**, such as:
```
FTP_PROXY=http://<proxy-host>[:<proxy-port>]
HTTPS_PROXY=http://<proxy-host>[:<proxy-port>]
HTTP_PROXY=http://<proxy-host>[:<proxy-port>]
NO_PROXY=.<private-domain>,localhost,127.0.0.1,0.0.0.0,::1
ftp_proxy=http://proxy-host/[:proxy-port]
http_proxy=http://proxy-host/[:proxy-port]
https_proxy=http://proxy-host/[:proxy-port]
no_proxy=.<private-domain>,localhost,127.0.0.1,0.0.0.0,::1
```

1. Install any necessary SSL inspection CA certificates and update CA trust system wide:
```
    sudo install -o root -g root -m 0644 *.crt /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/
    sudo update-ca-trust
```
2. Configure proxy variables for **rpm-ostree** SystemD service:
```
    sudo install -o root -g root -m 0644 proxy.env /etc/proxy.env
```
4. Ensure **rpm-ostreed** SystemD service uses a proxy to download the QEMU support package:
```
    sudo mkdir /etc/systemd/system/rpm-ostreed.service.d
    sudo install -o root -g root -m 0644 rpm-ostreed.conf /etc/systemd/system/rpm-ostreed.service.d/
    sudo systemctl daemon-reload
    sudo systemctl restart rpm-ostreed.service
```
5. Determine if emulation package is installed (exit status 0 means it is present, stdout will have full package name; non-zero otherwise):
```
    rpm -q qemu-user-static
```
6. Install emulation package if needed (this takes awhile, not due to connectivity but due to FCOS processing):
```
    sudo rpm-ostree install qemu-user-static
    sudo systemctl reboot
```

Avoid rebooting inside qemu, podman wont do things like mounting your volumes when it comes back up. Rely on the machine stop and start commands.
```
podman machine stop
podman machine start
```

Podman perfectly re-mounts any volumes upon start up regardless of how the managed machine is terminated (e.g., `podman machine stop`, `pkill -9 qemu`, `podman machine ssh sudo systemctl poweroff` or even for a one-stop shop for restarting `podman machine ssh sudo systemctl reboot`). I have yet to see a problem in regard to Podman mounting volumes. This is working for hundreds of people for my work across both Intel and M1 Mac's.

Curious why you state to "avoid rebooting inside qemu" and "podman wont do things like mounting your volumes when it comes back up." Because this is a behavior that I do not see (volumes are always remounted without issue on start).

Now, I am no QEMU expert by far but...

Here is what actually happens when you issue `podman machine stop`: A QEMU monitor command is sent to the VM to shutdown the VM gracefully. This is done by sending the command through a *nix socket (aka named pipe, and more formally a FIFO file - IPC 101). The QEMU monitor listens on for commands on its receiving end. The QEMU monitor command that is sent is `system_powerdown` and you can see this fact here where it is [setup](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/machine/qemu/machine.go#L729) and here where it is actually [executed](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/pkg/machine/qemu/machine.go#L749).

Simple help from the QEMU monitor service itself on this command:
```
QEMU 7.0.0 monitor - type 'help' for more information
(qemu) help system_powerdown 
system_powerdown  -- send system power down event
```
Meanwhile more information on the [QEMU monitor](https://qemu-project.gitlab.io/qemu/system/monitor.htmlhttps://qemu-project.gitlab.io/qemu/system/monitor.html) and everything that is available.

Notice that the VM will be powered down (if supported) and it is an event request that gets queued to QEMU which then will dequeue the request and oblige. Meanwhile Podman is monitoring the QEMU process by PID via the PID file it creates and when it discovers that the process no longer exists then it considers the VM to be "stopped",.

If I am not mistaken the QEMU monitor command `system_powerdown` winds up posting an ACPI event which then is handled by SystemD or the power management of FCOS (and pretty much every other modern operating system out there). The O/S then obliges by gracefully shutting down the system. When we SSH to the Podman VM and issue a shutdown or reboot via `systemctl` we are doing the same equivalent of `podman machine stop` with the exception that we entirely ignore the PID file and talking to the QEMU monitor. (and remember `socat` is your friend).

Would you like to know more? https://cpolizzi.github.io/podman/enabling-qemu-cli-monitoring-and-logging.html

And while we're at it (just because and in case you need to): https://cpolizzi.github.io/podman/orchestrating-podman-vm-to-host-communication.html

I will get to a little later tying the previous into enabling QEMU emulation when running in a restrictive security enclave environment....

> Yes we are working on this upstream right now, basically trying to get qemu-user-static split into separate packages so we could install fewer of them on Fedora CoreOS.
> 
> https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2061584

Note that this is done now and the `qemu-user-static-x86` package will be in the next `testing` and `next` Fedora CoreOS release on aarch64. Follow https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1088 for updates.

A TL;DR for people like me for whom it wasn't immediately clear how to use the solution from this issue. Here's how this works on a x86 Mac.

An initial attempt to run aarch64 architecture while on x86 will give you an error:

```
$ podman run --rm --arch aarch64 -it alpine
{"msg":"exec container process `/bin/sh`: Exec format error","level":"error","time":"2022-09-25T00:59:46.000783556Z"}
```

In order to fix it, you do the following:

```
$ podman machine ssh
# Inside the machine
$ sudo rpm-ostree install qemu-user-static-aarch64
$ sudo systemctl reboot
```

Now you just wait for the machine to come back up, and voila:

```
podman run --rm --arch aarch64 -it alpine
/ #
```

In case you're on MacOS with M1 and want to run x86 containers, you instead install `qemu-user-static-x86` on your podman machine.

> In case you're on MacOS with M1 and want to run x86 containers, you instead install `qemu-user-static-x86` on your podman machine.

In that case there is no need since `qemu-user-static-x86` is already installed on `aarch64` FCOS. See https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1088 and https://github.com/coreos/fedora-coreos-tracker/issues/1237.
Yep, in my case on an ARM MacOS, the default `podman machine init` machine had this built in. I just had to remember to use `--arch amd64` to my build/run commands.
